argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the true nature and origin of morality — and why does conventional morality deserve to be questioned?
argumentCard.thesis: The dominant moral systems of the West — Christian ethics and Kantian universalism — are expressions of weakness and resentment disguised as objective truth. A genuine revaluation of values, led by the 'free spirit,' is both possible and necessary.
keyMoves: Attacks the dogmatic assumption that morality is self-evident or universal, showing that every moral system reflects the interests and psychology of those who created it

Distinguishes 'master morality' (rooted in nobility, self-affirmation, and the creation of values) from 'slave morality' (rooted in resentment of the strong and the inversion of their values into 'evil')
Argues that the will to power — not happiness, utility, or duty — is the fundamental drive behind all human action and value-creation
Calls for a new kind of philosopher — the 'philosopher of the future' — who creates values rather than merely systematizing received ones
argumentCard.strongestObjection: Nietzsche's framework risks self-refutation: if all morality is a expression of power, then Nietzsche's own 'revaluation' is just another bid for dominance. His contempt for 'herd morality' also provides rhetorical cover for cruelty, as its historical appropriations tragically demonstrated.

debates.title: Is morality a universal law or an expression of power?
debates.subtitle: Nietzsche's genealogy vs. Kant's categorical imperative

philosopher1.name: Friedrich Nietzsche
philosopher1.position: Morality is a historical weapon — not universal truth
philosopher1.keyQuote: What is good? — Everything that heightens the feeling of power, the will to power, power itself in man.
arguments: So-called 'universal morality' is not universal at all — it is the revenge of the weak on the strong. Christian and democratic morality teaches the powerful to be ashamed of their strength.
Kant's categorical imperative — act only according to principles you could universalize — disguises a deep desire for conformity and mediocrity. It is the ethics of the herd.
The great creative individuals — artists, philosophers, conquerors of spirit — have always broken with conventional morality. Morality as universal law is the enemy of human greatness.

philosopher2.name: Immanuel Kant
philosopher2.position: Morality is grounded in pure reason — and applies to all rational beings
philosopher2.keyQuote: Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.
arguments: Morality cannot be grounded in power or desire — these are contingent and variable. Only reason provides the unconditional foundation that moral law requires.
Every rational being is an end in themselves, never merely a means. This is not weakness — it is the recognition of what makes human beings worthy of respect.

Nietzsche's 'will to power' simply re-labels selfishness as virtue. Any ethical system that celebrates the strong dominating the weak cannot be consistently defended without exempting oneself from its consequences.

debates.stakes: This is not merely an academic dispute. Nietzsche's ideas were appropriated to justify authoritarianism; Kant's were used to ground human rights. The stakes concern whether moral equality is a deep truth or a comfortable fiction.
debates.yourTurn: Is Nietzsche diagnosing something real about how moral systems work in practice — even if his solution is dangerous? Or does Kant have the stronger foundation? Can you have Nietzsche's insight without his risks?
